skill.name: git-workflow
skill.description: Guide for Git branch naming, worktrees, conventional commits, rebasing, stashing, pull requests, and cleanup.
skill.summary: Standardize Git work with worktrees, issues, commits, pull requests, and cleanup steps.
skill.author: Barnhardt-Enterprises-Inc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, crypto, sensitive file, and network patterns. Review found no malicious code or prompt injection; most alerts are Markdown examples or false positives. The skill still teaches commands that can change repositories, worktrees, branches, and GitHub state, so it should publish with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Mutating Git Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The external command findings are true positives for operational risk, not malware. The skill provides Git and GitHub CLI commands that can create worktrees, add files, commit, rebase, push branches, create pull requests, remove worktrees, delete branches, and modify stashes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit Markdown examples and match the skill purpose. They are not auto-executed, but an AI agent following them can change local and remote repository state.

medium_findings.title: Parent-Directory Worktree Paths
medium_findings.description: The path traversal findings are false positives for malicious traversal, but the documented worktree paths intentionally use parent-directory references. If copied without checking the current directory, cleanup commands could target the wrong worktree path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory paths are visible workflow examples, not hidden path traversal logic. The residual risk is operational because remove and cd commands depend on the caller's current directory.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution alerts are Markdown code fences and inline command examples, not Ruby execution. The weak cryptography alert has no supporting cryptographic code, the .gitconfig line is an alias example, and the network reconnaissance alert is a protected-branch rule.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows documentation text and fenced examples only. No executable Ruby, cryptographic implementation, sensitive file access, or network scanning behavior was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Git and GitHub CLI Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes bash examples for git worktree, add, commit, push, rebase, stash, branch deletion, and gh issue or pull request creation. These commands are expected for the workflow but can alter local and remote state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are direct shell commands in Markdown. Their effects are clear, but there is no evidence of hidden execution or command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Relative Filesystem Targets for Worktrees
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples use ../worktrees paths and worktree removal commands. This is a common Git worktree layout, but it requires current-directory verification before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path pattern is intentional documentation for Git worktrees. It is safe as guidance, but risky if executed from an unexpected directory.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when branches, commits, worktrees, and pull requests follow different patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable Git workflow for issues, commits, rebases, and cleanup.
actual_capabilities: Defines branch naming patterns for features, fixes, refactors, documentation, and tests.
Shows conventional commit formats with scopes and breaking change markers.
Provides Git worktree commands for creating, listing, removing, and pruning worktrees.
Outlines an issue-to-pull-request workflow using GitHub CLI commands.
Documents rebase, stash, cleanup, and useful Git alias examples.
limitations: It is a Markdown guide, not an automated Git tool.
Commands require Git and GitHub CLI to be installed and authenticated.
Examples use placeholder branch names, issue numbers, and worktree paths.
It does not validate repository policy or prevent destructive Git operations.

use_cases.title: Prepare Feature Branches
use_cases.description: Create consistent branches and worktrees before starting new issue work.

use_cases.title: Clean Commit History
use_cases.description: Use conventional commits and rebasing to prepare readable pull requests.

use_cases.title: Document Team Git Standards
use_cases.description: Give contributors a concise workflow for branches, commits, pull requests, and cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Name My Branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-workflow skill to suggest a branch name for issue 42: add user authentication.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Worktree
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-workflow skill to plan the commands for a new worktree for issue 57, a login redirect fix.

prompt_templates.title: Review Commit Messages
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-workflow skill to rewrite these commit ideas as conventional commits: login form, auth API, auth tests.

prompt_templates.title: Guide a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-workflow skill to outline the issue, worktree, commit, rebase, push, pull request, and cleanup steps for issue 63.

output_examples.input: I need a branch for issue 42 about user authentication.
output_examples.output: Use a feature branch with the issue number and a short slug. A suitable name is feature/42-user-authentication.

output_examples.input: My branch has several messy commits before review.
output_examples.output: Fetch the latest main branch, rebase your branch, then use interactive rebase to squash or reorder commits before pushing.

output_examples.input: What should I do after my worktree branch is merged?
output_examples.output: Return to the main repository, remove the feature worktree, delete the merged local branch, and prune stale worktree metadata.

best_practices: Confirm the current directory before running worktree removal commands.
Use issue numbers in branch names to keep work traceable.
Review branch protection rules before pushing or rebasing shared branches.
anti_patterns: Do not run cleanup commands against paths you have not verified.
Do not force push to protected or shared branches without team approval.
Do not copy placeholder issue numbers or branch names into real work.

faq.question: Does this skill run Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and command examples for an AI assistant or user to apply.

faq.question: Does it require GitHub?
faq.answer: Some examples use GitHub CLI for issues and pull requests. Core branch and worktree guidance works with Git alone.

faq.question: Can it help with conventional commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives formats for commit types, scoped commits, and breaking change markers.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Use review before execution. The examples can change branches, worktrees, commits, stashes, and remote pull requests.

faq.question: Can it create branch protection rules?
faq.answer: No. It lists recommended protected branch rules but does not configure repository settings.

faq.question: What tools work with this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
